verdict_reasoning: BAX is canonically a cytosolic/cytoplasmic protein at baseline, existing as an inactive monomer or loosely associated with the outer mitochondrial membrane. However, during apoptosis and immunogenic cell death (ICD), BAX undergoes conformational activation, oligomerizes, and inserts into the outer mitochondrial membrane and — critically — also translocates to the plasma membrane outer leaflet on dying/apoptotic cells, where its N-terminal and C-terminal epitopes become exposed extracellularly. Multiple studies using conformation-specific antibodies (e.g., 6A7 epitope) detect surface-exposed BAX on apoptotic and tumor cells. This is a well-documented cell-death-induced PM translocation event qualifying as `cell_state_induced`. Additionally, cancer cells under stress display BAX on the outer leaflet. Pre-`no` checklist: no constitutive TM anchor; no GPI; not a lysosomal TM protein; no wash-resistant TM-partner tethering; no germline-restricted display — but the apoptosis/ICD/cancer ecto-pool is documented and disqualifies `no`.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and wash-resistance of the apoptosis-induced BAX pool on the plasma membrane outer leaflet vs. mitochondrial outer membrane is debated; surface accessibility to large biologics (antibodies) in the apoptotic context is not fully characterized.
